citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Female beagle, Freda, was examined by the Attending Veterinarian on 09/29/2025. The dog was also tested for
heartworm during the exam, and the results of the test were positive. The licensee stated that he did discuss treatment
options with the veterinarian, but no treatment was ever provided to the dog. Heartworm disease can have a negative
impact on the health of the dog, and if left untreated, could even result in death.
Each dealer shall use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee
must consult with a veterinarian to develop an effective treatment plan for Freda's heartworm disease. The licensee must
ensure that each dog is provided with adequate veterinary care at all times.
Correction date: The facility representative was verbally instructed to contact the veterinarian by end of business on
February 13, 2026.
The vaccines for the dogs are stored in their own drawer inside the refrigerator. There is one box in the drawer containing
vaccines for Leptospirosis and DAPPv. The Leptospirosis vaccine expired March 16, 2023, and the DAPPv expired
October 05, 2022. These expired vaccines are comingled with vaccines that are current and, therefore, are considered
ready for use. The use of expired vaccines does not ensure that the dogs are adequately protected from dangerous and
deadly diseases.
Each dealer shall use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee
must either dispose of all such expired vaccines or segregate them in an appropriately labeled, physically separate
location from non-expired vaccines to ensure they are not mistakenly used. The licensee must ensure that each dog
receives adequate veterinary care at all times.
Correction date: Beginning February 13, 2026, and onward all vaccines given to the dogs must not be expired.

There are 2 bags of unopened dog food and 3 bags of unopened bedding material stored directly on the floor of the
housing facility. Food and bedding stored on the floor could become spoiled or contaminated by spilled liquids or by pests
such as rodents.
Supplies of food and bedding must be stored in a manner that protects the supplies from spoilage, contamination, and
vermin infestation. These supplies must be stored off the floor and away from the walls. The licensee must store the
unopened bags of food and bedding off the floor and away from the walls in order to protect these supplies from any
potential contamination.
Correction date: February 26, 2026

The trash container in the housing facility has no lid. Exposed trash could create odors and attract pests such as flies and
rodents which could increase the risk of disease hazards towards the dogs.
Trash containers in housing facilities must have tightly fitted lids on them at all times. The licensee must add a tightly fitted
lid to the trash can and ensure the lid remains in place at all times.
Correction date: February 26, 2026

Each enclosure contains an access door that allows the dogs to move between the indoor and outdoor sections of their
enclosure. A corner of the metal frame around one access door has bent upward and is no longer flush against the wall. It
has created a sharp point that is located at the level of the dogs. Two dogs are housed in this affected enclosure. Contact
with sharp points could cause injury to the dogs.
Primary enclosures must be maintained so that they protect the dogs from injury. The licensee must remove this sharp
point or alter it in a manner that eliminates the risk of injury to the dogs. The licensee must ensure the enclosures are safe
for the dogs at all times.
Correction date: February 26, 2026
Metal livestock fencing has been buried under the dirt and gravel flooring of the outdoor section of the enclosures. In at
least four enclosures, containing a total of 7 dogs, the dirt and gravel flooring has settled exposing the underground
fencing and, in some areas, the fencing now sits off the ground. In order to move around their enclosures, the dogs must
either walk on the metal rods of the fencing or step in the openings between the metal rods. This exposed fencing could
cause injury to the feet of the dogs as they walk on the metal rods or when stepping into the fence openings.
Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they protect the dogs from injury. The licensee must
either cover the exposed fencing with compliant flooring material or alter the exposed fencing in a manner that eliminates
the risk of injury to the dogs. The licensee must ensure the enclosures are safe for the dogs at all times.
Correction date: March 5, 2026

Two dogs have no water in their water bucket. Failure to provide potable water at all times could have a negative impact
on the health of the dogs should they become dehydrated.
Potable water must be continuously available to the dogs, unless restricted by the attending veterinarian. The licensee
must ensure that each dog has access to potable water at all times in order to protect their health and well-being.
Correction date: The facility representative provided the dogs with water at the time of the inspection.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The housing facility contains 16 adult dogs and the facility representative stated that the enclosures are only sanitized
twice a year using bleach. There is a buildup of dirt on the access doors, the access door frames, and the exterior walls of
the enclosures. A dark brown buildup of food debris is coating the interiors of the food buckets and self-feeders. The
interior of at least on metal water bucket, accessed by two dogs, is coated in a buildup of a wet, brown-colored organic
material. Failure to sanitize surfaces and the buildup of dirt, debris, and waste could increase the risk of disease hazards
towards the dogs.
Used primary enclosures and food and water receptacles for dogs must be sanitized once every two weeks or more often
to prevent the buildup of dirt, debris, food waste and excreta. The licensee must ensure that all of the enclosure surfaces,
including food and water buckets, remain free of a buildup of dirt, debris, and waste materials and are sanitized at least
every two weeks in order to support the health of the dogs.
Correction date: February 26, 2026

The Program of Veterinary Care could not be located by the licensee; therefore, the inspector was unable to review it for
compliance. Failure to provide the Program of Veterinary Care could interfere with the ability of APHIS officials to
determine if the dogs are receiving adequate veterinary care.
Each dealer must keep and maintain the written program of veterinary care and make it available for APHIS inspection.
The licensee must ensure that all required documents and information are available for inspection.
Correction date: February 23, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Female beagle, Freda, was examined by the Attending Veterinarian on 09/29/2025. The dog was also tested for

Each dealer shall use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee 
must consult with a veterinarian to develop an effective treatment plan for Freda's heartworm disease. The licensee must 
ensure that each dog is provided with adequate veterinary care at all times. 
 
Correction date: The facility representative was verbally instructed to contact the veterinarian by end of business on 
February 13, 2026. 
 
The vaccines for the dogs are stored in their own drawer inside the refrigerator. There is one box in the drawer containing

October 05, 2022. These expired vaccines are comingled with vaccines that are current and, therefore, are considered

Each dealer shall use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. The licensee 
must either dispose of all such expired vaccines or segregate them in an appropriately labeled, physically separate 
location from non-expired vaccines to ensure they are not mistakenly used. The licensee must ensure that each dog 
receives adequate veterinary care at all times. 
 
Correction date: Beginning February 13, 2026, and onward all vaccines given to the dogs must not be expired. 
3.1(e) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
There are 2 bags of unopened dog food and 3 bags of unopened bedding material stored directly on the floor of the 
housing facility. Food and bedding stored on the floor could become spoiled or contaminated by spilled liquids or by pests

such as rodents. 
 
Supplies of food and bedding must be stored in a manner that protects the supplies from spoilage, contamination, and 
vermin infestation. These supplies must be stored off the floor and away from the walls. The licensee must store the 
unopened bags of food and bedding off the floor and away from the walls in order to protect these supplies from any

Correction date: February 26, 2026 
3.1(f) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
The trash container in the housing facility has no lid. Exposed trash could create odors and attract pests such as flies and 
rodents which could increase the risk of disease hazards towards the dogs.  
 
Trash containers in housing facilities must have tightly fitted lids on them at all times. The licensee must add a tightly fitted 
lid to the trash can and ensure the lid remains in place at all times. 
 
Correction date: February 26, 2026 
3.6(a)(2)(ii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
Each enclosure contains an access door that allows the dogs to move between the indoor and outdoor sections of their

has created a sharp point that is located at the level of the dogs. Two dogs are housed in this affected enclosure. Contact

Primary enclosures must be maintained so that they protect the dogs from injury. The licensee must remove this sharp 
point or alter it in a manner that eliminates the risk of injury to the dogs. The licensee must ensure the enclosures are safe 
for the dogs at all times. 
 
Correction date: February 26, 2026 
 
Metal livestock fencing has been buried under the dirt and gravel flooring of the outdoor section of the enclosures. In at 
least four enclosures, containing a total of 7 dogs, the dirt and gravel flooring has settled exposing the underground 
fencing and, in some areas, the fencing now sits off the ground. In order to move around their enclosures, the dogs must 
either walk on the metal rods of the fencing or step in the openings between the metal rods. This exposed fencing could 
cause injury to the feet of the dogs as they walk on the metal rods or when stepping into the fence openings.  
 
Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they protect the dogs from injury. The licensee must 
either cover the exposed fencing with compliant flooring material or alter the exposed fencing in a manner that eliminates 
the risk of injury to the dogs. The licensee must ensure the enclosures are safe for the dogs at all times.

Correction date: March 5, 2026 
3.10(a) 
   
Watering 
Two dogs have no water in their water bucket. Failure to provide potable water at all times could have a negative impact 
on the health of the dogs should they become dehydrated. 
 
Potable water must be continuously available to the dogs, unless restricted by the attending veterinarian. The licensee 
must ensure that each dog has access to potable water at all times in order to protect their health and well-being. 
 
Correction date: The facility representative provided the dogs with water at the time of the inspection. 
3.11(b)(2) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The housing facility contains 16 adult dogs and the facility representative stated that the enclosures are only sanitized

Used primary enclosures and food and water receptacles for dogs must be sanitized once every two weeks or more often

Correction date: February 26, 2026 
3.13(a) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The Program of Veterinary Care could not be located by the licensee; therefore, the inspector was unable to review it for 
compliance. Failure to provide the Program of Veterinary Care could interfere with the ability of APHIS officials to

Each dealer must keep and maintain the written program of veterinary care and make it available for APHIS inspection. 
The licensee must ensure that all required documents and information are available for inspection.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
